lines.text: The front steps of Ridgemont High rise up like a dare. Somewhere behind Maya, a car pulls away. She doesn't turn to watch it go.
Okay so — new plan. I'm not 'new girl who cried in the car,' I'm 'new girl who's mysterious and has places to be.'
Very chill. Very normal. Ten out of ten normal. Sure. Nailed it already.
Right. Armor's on.
lines.action: straightens jacket collar

lines.text: Inside, the hallway is a river already moving — everyone flowing toward somewhere they've been a hundred times before. Maya catches her reflection in the trophy case.
Find a table. Don't cry. Befriend at least one (1) person. Boom. Day one, complete.
Okay. Ten minutes. Let's find out who I am today.
Room 114 is supposed to be right here. According to the schedule. According to the map she's already memorized. It is decidedly not.
Okay, locker two-forty... two-forty... you are testing me right now—
lines.action: turning in a slow circle

lines.text: Okay, spinning-in-circles girl, your locker's the one you're currently *not* standing in front of. Groundbreaking detective work.
A couple of nearby kids laugh. Jordan doesn't slow down long enough to see if it landed.
Cool. Cool, cool. Thank you, detective.
Cool locker technique. Very avant-garde.
lines.action: doesn't look up

lines.text: It's dry, and it's funny, and it's gone before Jordan even clocks it happened.
The cafeteria hits like a wall of sound. Maya's tray suddenly weighs approximately one thousand pounds.
Ferreira! Right? Come sit, come sit — we don't bite. Mostly.
lines.action: arm already half-raised, not quite looking

lines.text: A few tables over, Sam eats alone by the window, headphones on, eyes fixed on his tray like it's the only fixed point in the room.

Field report: window table's empty except for one very committed loner, popular table's already recruiting, and you have — *checks imaginary watch* — about four seconds before this becomes a whole thing.
lines.action: appearing out of nowhere

lines.text: No pressure.
Zero pressure. Massive, life-altering, completely-not-a-big-deal pressure.
Jordan's table runs like a live show — everyone half-performing, half-watching everyone else perform.
—and THEN he says 'I thought this was the poetry unit,' in front of the ENTIRE class—
Okay but seriously — you're funny. You're staying. This is decided.
lines.action: arm sliding along Maya's chair

lines.text: I mean — I wasn't gonna fight you on it.
lines.emotion: a little louder than she means to

lines.text: Sam passes with a full tray, headphones on, trying to be invisible. Jordan's foot slides out. 'As a joke.'
The tray hits the floor first. Sam hits the bench second. The table erupts.
Oh my god— Okafor, you good?
Everyone looks at Maya. Waiting to see which way she'll go.
Hey — that wasn't cool.
lines.action: helping Sam gather his tray

lines.text: Relax, it was a joke.
It doesn't quite land. Jordan knows it too.
Thanks.
lines.action: mouths it silently, slipping toward the trash cans

lines.text: For the rest of lunch, Jordan's jokes carry a fraction less warmth aimed Maya's way. It doesn't fix everything. It isn't nothing.
The laugh is out of Maya's mouth before she decides to let it out.
See, this one gets it.
lines.action: arm sliding along the back of Maya's chair like a prize

lines.text: Sam doesn't look back on his way to the trash cans. Somehow that's worse than if he had.
So — anyway, does anyone else think the cafeteria pizza tastes like the box it came in?
Oh my god, RIGHT? It's like eating cardboard's cardboard.
The table's attention slides right off the tray on the floor. Sam picks himself up without a word. Nobody quite manages to look at him while he does it. Maya included.
You know the popular table's like, right there. Very much still an option.
Yeah, I know where it is. I have functioning eyes.
lines.action: sitting anyway

lines.text: Noted.
For the record, that pizza's not food. It's a legal loophole.
lines.action: nodding at her tray

lines.text: Oh no. What's actually good here.
Nothing. That's the tragedy. The vending machine has three flavors of 'regret' and calls it a candy selection. It's basically a personal attack.
Okay, you have opinions.
I've had a lot of lunch periods to develop them.
You always sit here?
Used to sit with a bigger group. People got... busy.
lines.action: shrug, eyes on his tray

lines.text: He says it like a fact, not a wound. It doesn't take a genius to hear the part he skipped.
Today, huh?
...Yeah. Okay. There's a table by the art room, actually. They're weird in a good way.
lines.action: nodding toward it

lines.text: It started as Maya's suggestion. By the end, it's entirely his call.
Neither of them says much else. It isn't awkward — it's easy, in the way only two people who've stopped performing for anyone can be.

lines.action: slides a sketchbook across the table without a word — a half-finished comic panel, sharp and funny in a way his quiet voice never hints at

lines.text: You draw?
I mean. Sometimes.
lines.action: shrug, like it's not a big deal — it clearly is

lines.text: ...Hey.
lines.action: waving two kids over from a nearby table

lines.text: Sit with us.
Whatever door just opened, he's the one who opened it.
Hey, I'm Alex — they/them. Third new school in four years, so I've got the introduction down to a science.
lines.action: dropping into the seat like gravity decided for them

lines.text: That obvious I'm new?
You've got the exact haunted look I had in sixth grade. Very 'where is the bathroom and also who am I now.' Said with love.
That tracks.
Okay but real talk — building a table from scratch is honestly the power move. Nobody assigned you a seat. You just... claimed one.
lines.action: rearranging napkins into a neat grid

lines.text: Feels less like claiming and more like nobody else wanted it.
Wow, low bar, we're already doing the vulnerable honesty bit, love that for us.
So then I figured — what if there was a club that was just, like, complaining about the cafeteria pizza. Formally. Minutes taken. Real bylaws.
That's either the best idea I've ever heard or a cry for help.
Extremely possible it's both.
Wait — okay, we're doing this, hold on—
lines.action: already on their feet, waving over two kids from the lunch line

lines.text: You two. Sit. We're starting a table and possibly a club, no pressure.
By the bell, their table of two is a table of five. Alex looks like they just won something.
...okay but real talk,
I'm kind of tired of always being the one who makes the first move. I'm good at starting over. I'd just like a place I don't have to.
You don't have to today.
It's not sadness, exactly — just honesty, sitting still for a second before the conversation moves on.
You can want both things, you know.
lines.action: catching Maya's eyes drift toward Jordan's table

lines.text: Doesn't make you a fraud.
...I wasn't— okay, maybe a little.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park — you three. Congratulations, you get each other for the semester. Try to enjoy it.
lines.action: tapping clipboard twice

lines.text: Perfect. I have thoughts. Several.
lines.action: already flipping to a prepped page

lines.text: Great.
lines.action: jaw tight, not looking at Maya

lines.text: Jordan catches Maya's eye across the room for half a second — like he's trying to read which version of today just happened.
Given the last hour. Can I really blame him.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park. Congratulations, you get each other for the semester.
lines.action: tapping clipboard twice

lines.text: Oh, hey.
Try to keep up.
lines.action: flipping to a prepped page

lines.text: We'll manage.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park.
lines.action: tapping clipboard twice

lines.text: Sam. That's — the quiet kid by the window, right?
Great, let's not waste the period.
lines.action: already flipping to a fresh page before his name finishes being read

lines.text: Good luck!
lines.action: mouthing it across the room with an exaggerated shrug

lines.text: Okay, hear me out — this is basically what my brother did two years ago, and it got him an A. I'm not messing with a formula that works.
lines.action: talking with his hands

lines.text: Or — we could do something visual. Like a fake news broadcast instead of a poster. It's not that different, just—
Respectfully, that's not going to work, we don't have the equipment and we don't have the—
lines.action: talking over him

lines.text: We have phones. That's the equipment.
I can't just do something different and hope it works. I'm not doing worse than him.
For half a second the certainty cracks all the way through. Then it's gone, replaced by his usual sureness. Maya caught it. So did Sam.
Anyway. Point is—
...Fine. Yeah. Maybe I got in my head about it.

lines.action: sits up a little straighter, doesn't say anything, doesn't need to

lines.text: It was never really about being defended. It was about being heard.
What if we did some of both — the broadcast idea, but structured the way your brother's project was?
...that could actually work. If we keep the structure tight.
Yeah. I can work with that.
Nobody's thrilled. Everybody can work with it. For a first day, that might be its own small win.
Right, so — poster, three sections, I'll take the intro.
Sam doesn't say another word for the rest of the period.
It wasn't a big deal. It wasn't. ...It kind of was.
Whatever's waiting for you at home, Devon — this project doesn't have to be a rerun of somebody else's. It can just be yours.
Devon doesn't have a comeback for that. He nods once, and heads out.
Huh. Didn't expect that to be the sentence that got him.
Backpack on. Day survived. Bus loop, late afternoon.
Ten minutes felt like a year this morning. Now the whole day's folding down into a backpack-sized thing.
Maya's phone buzzes.
Okay. Last five minutes of day one. No pressure.
The reply comes back fast — an exclamation point Sam clearly doesn't use often.
Wait you actually texted me!! I mean — cool. Cool cool cool. What's up.
Yeah. Same table tomorrow?
lines.emotion: small smile she doesn't bother hiding

lines.text: Obviously. Who else is gonna explain the vending machine tragedy to you.
Jordan's group chat pings — plans already forming for tomorrow, Maya's name already on the list.
ferreira's in obviously. who else
Underneath it, unopened: a text from Sam. 'good luck with the group project tmrw, lol.'
I'll answer that. Later. Probably.
what if tomorrow's table has room for like, everybody. even jordan if he can chill for five minutes
lines.emotion: text message, typed fast before she can talk herself out of it

lines.text: oh i am WILDLY into this. bringing napkins for the seating chart. see you at 7:58.

options.text: Wow, okay, we're doing bits already? Love that for you.
Rough first day for everyone, huh?
Ha — yeah, very smooth. Very me.

a.action: a small, private almost-smile, gone fast

b.action: quick glance at Maya, surprised someone noticed

c.text: See, this one gets it. Come on.
c.action: arm sliding around Maya's shoulders, already moving on
eyes drop, steps back into the crowd

options.text: Yeah — okay, I'm coming.
Think I'll sit over there.
I think I'm good right here, actually.

a.action: already scooting over to make room

b.action: sliding his tray over a little

options.text: Hey — that wasn't cool.
(Laugh along with the table.)
So — anyway, does anyone else think the pizza tastes like the box?

b.action: arm sliding along the back of Maya's chair

c.text: Oh my god, RIGHT? It's like eating cardboard's cardboard.

options.text: Honestly? We'd probably both do better making new friends. Starting today.
I'm good right here, if that's cool.
Okay but — you should wave somebody over. Right now.

b.action: small nod, like that was the right answer

options.text: Okay, we need more people at this table. Immediately.
Honestly, I'm kind of into just this.
...Okay, not gonna lie, I keep looking over there.

b.text: Oh thank god. My legs were not ready to stand back up.

c.text: You can want both things, you know. Doesn't make you a fraud.

options.text: Wait, I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying.
What if we did some of both?
(Say nothing. Let it go.)

a.text: ...Fine. Yeah. Maybe I got in my head about it.

b.text: ...that could actually work. If we keep the structure tight.

c.text: Right, so — poster, three sections, I'll take the intro.

options.text: hey. same table tomorrow?
yeah, I'm in
ok wild idea — what if tomorrow's table had room for everybody

a.emotion: text message, exclamation point he clearly doesn't use often

b.text: knew it. see you at the good table

c.text: oh i am WILDLY into this

lines.text: One person. Not the whole hallway. Just — one real one.
Not bad for a first day, Ferreira.
also. i think devon's brother's project was actually kind of overrated. don't tell him i said that.
It felt good, back there. Loud good. Easy good.
I laughed too, though. When the tray went down. I didn't have to.
Later.
lines.action: thumb hovering over Sam's text, then locking the phone

lines.text: I did stand up for him. At lunch. That has to count for something.
So why does going back to that table feel like taking it back.
yeah, in
lines.emotion: typing to Jordan's chat instead of answering Sam

lines.text: Nothing bad happened today. Nothing really happened at all.
That's supposed to feel like winning.
Tomorrow. I'll figure out tomorrow.
lines.action: locks the phone without answering either text

lines.text: I didn't pick a side today. Turns out that was the point.
...I'll believe it when I see Jordan share a table without an audience. But sure. I'm in.

voiceNotes: Jordan speaks in performance mode almost constantly — even one-on-one lines carry an implied audience, which is why her 'relax, it was a joke' in the defend-aftermath is the first crack: she's trying to convince herself as much as Maya. Sam's armor is precision and understatement; his real feeling always shows up sideways, in a joke that's slightly too specific to be casual ('legal loophole,' 'personal attack'). He only drops the deflection fully in the quiet-aftermath sketchbook beat — that's the tell that trust has actually been earned, not just offered. Devon's crack in scene-6 ('I'm not doing worse than him') should read as involuntary — faster, tighter, no hand gestures for that one line only, then the hands come back as he recovers. Alex is the only character who names things directly ('you can want both things') — they've done this new-kid routine enough times to have dropped some of the armor everyone else is still wearing. Ms. Alvarez never over-explains; her one line to Devon should land and then the scene should get out of the way.

alternatives: Locker's not gonna find itself, but nice moves.
Okay but as a bit, ten out of ten. As directions, zero.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Jordan's line performative and audience-aware while offering slightly less/more edge depending on how sharp the studio wants the first tease to land.

alternatives: Group kind of... thinned out. It's whatever.
Had a bigger crew last year. Schedules happen.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Preserves the underplayed, fact-not-wound delivery if 'got busy' reads as too on-the-nose in voice direction.

alternatives: I'm not doing worse than him. I can't be the one who does worse.
It has to be at least as good. It just does.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Slightly more raw phrasing if the studio wants Devon's crack to land harder before he recovers his composure.
